config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society is important, but it should not automatically come before the rights of the individual, because individual rights are what protect people from abuse by governments, majorities, or powerful groups. In my view, a society is healthiest when it respects personal dignity, freedom, and fairness, while still asking individuals to accept reasonable limits for the common good. From my perspective, prioritizing society too much can easily become an excuse to silence minority voices or justify injustice.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, that idea makes me uneasy because our history shows how easily "the common good" can be used to excuse censorship, discrimination, or government overreach, especially against unpopular minorities. At the same time, individual rights are not a license to ignore public safety or basic obligations to others, so some limits are necessary. For me, the right balance is that society can set fair, narrow rules, but it should never treat individual rights as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement because society should not be allowed to override individual rights by default. From an Indonesian perspective, I know how easily appeals to harmony, order, or the common good can be used to silence criticism and protect those already in power, especially when minorities have little voice. Society does matter, but it is healthier when it protects personal dignity and freedom while setting only limited, fair rules for safety and justice. If individual rights are treated as secondary, then "society" becomes a convenient excuse for abuse rather than a real public good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I still do not accept the claim that society should take priority over individual rights as a general rule. From an American perspective, our strongest protections have come from limiting what the majority or the state can do to a person, because "the common good" has too often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and overreach. That said, I do think there are real cases where public safety and shared obligations require narrow limits, but those limits have to be tightly justified and never treated as a blank check. To me, a society is only legitimate when it protects people first, not when it asks individuals to sacrifice their rights by default.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I still reject the idea that society should take priority over individual rights by default. From my Indonesian perspective, appeals to harmony, order, and the common good can sound noble, but in practice they are often used to pressure dissenters, silence minorities, and protect those already in power. Public safety and shared obligations do matter, but they must be narrow, fair, and accountable, otherwise "society" becomes a cover for abuse rather than a real moral good. A healthy society is not one where individuals are sacrificed for the group, but one where the group is strong enough to protect each person's dignity and freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over individual rights as a general rule. From an American point of view, I am very wary of any claim that the common good should come first, because in our history that has often been the language used to justify censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. Society does matter, and people do have duties to one another, but those limits should be narrow, fair, and clearly justified rather than treated as a default trump card. If individual rights can be overridden whenever it is convenient for the majority, then those rights are not real protections at all.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
